ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKR (Sports and energy drinks) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific zero-sugar isotonic drink code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one 325 ml can of 100 Plus Zero Sugar, which is the typical single-serve retail format sold in Singapore convenience stores and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: 100 Plus Zero Sugar — Nutrition Information Panel (F&N Foods Singapore)
nutrient_source.organization: F&N Foods Pte Ltd / USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 45226985 — analogous zero-sugar isotonic drink reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values derived from the declared Nutrition Information Panel on 100 Plus Zero Sugar packaging (F&N Foods Singapore), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central branded zero-sugar isotonic drink entries for secondary nutrient completeness. The product contains 0 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml as declared on the product label. Electrolyte values (sodium, potassium) are estimated from the declared label and USDA analogous entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sugar-Free Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: 100 Plus Zero Sugar contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml and therefore has no measurable glycemic response. Per University of Sydney GI Database convention, beverages with no glycemic carbohydrates are assigned GI = 1 (effectively zero) and classified as low GI. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: 100 Plus Zero Sugar contains approximately 46 mg sodium per 100 ml (approximately 150 mg per 325 ml can), which is modest and unlikely to meaningfully elevate blood pressure when consumed occasionally; individuals on strict sodium-restricted diets should account for this electrolyte contribution.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar and zero carbohydrate formulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: 100 Plus Zero Sugar contains no sugar or digestible carbohydrates and uses non-nutritive sweeteners (acesulfame K and sucralose), making it a suitable hydration option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who need to manage postprandial blood glucose; however, patients should be aware that some research suggests non-nutritive sweeteners may influence insulin sensitivity in susceptible individuals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero sugar, and low calorie profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: 100 Plus Zero Sugar contributes no dietary fat, cholesterol, or added sugar, and its low sodium content per serving poses minimal cardiovascular risk; it can serve as a calorie-free hydration alternative to sugar-sweetened beverages for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 100 Plus Zero Sugar is a commercially manufactured, sugar-free isotonic carbonated beverage produced by F&N Foods in Singapore. It is the zero-calorie variant of the iconic 100 Plus isotonic drink brand, which has been a staple sports and lifestyle beverage in Singapore and Southeast Asia since the 1980s. The drink is formulated with water, electrolytes (sodium citrate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride), citric acid, natural flavourings, and non-nutritive sweeteners (acesulfame K and sucralose) in place of sugar. It contains zero calories, zero grams of protein, fat, and carbohydrate per 100 ml, making it nutritionally equivalent to flavoured electrolyte water. The taste is lightly citrus-flavoured with a mild effervescence, delivering a refreshing and revitalising sensation similar to the original 100 Plus but without the sweetness of sugar. It is widely consumed as a post-exercise rehydration drink, a sugar-free alternative to sodas, and a general thirst-quencher in Singapore's hot and humid climate. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, obesity, or those on calorie-restricted diets, as it provides electrolyte replenishment without glycemic impact. The sodium content (~46 mg per 100 ml) supports mild electrolyte balance. It is halal-certified, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary groups in Singapore's multicultural population.
common_user_queries: Is 100 Plus Zero Sugar suitable for diabetics?
How many calories does 100 Plus Zero Sugar have?
What sweetener is used in 100 Plus Zero Sugar?
Can I drink 100 Plus Zero Sugar on a diet?
What is the sodium content of 100 Plus Zero Sugar?
Is 100 Plus Zero Sugar the same as the original 100 Plus?
Does 100 Plus Zero Sugar have carbohydrates?
Is 100 Plus Zero Sugar good for rehydration?
What is the GI of 100 Plus Zero Sugar?
meal_context: sugar-free beverage option at hawker centres and food courts
accompaniment to meals for calorie-conscious individuals

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from the declared product Nutrition Information Panel (F&N Foods Singapore) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central analogous branded zero-sugar isotonic drink entries. The product is a commercially standardised formulation with consistent declared values; however, no direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific branded product, so confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: Zero-macro justification: energy_kcal is confirmed zero per product label declaration — this is a zero-calorie beverage by formulation. protein_g contains no protein — confirmed zero per product label; this is a water-based electrolyte beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. fat_g contains no fat — confirmed zero by product formulation; no fat-containing ingredients are present. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per product label; sugar has been replaced entirely by non-nutritive sweeteners (acesulfame K and sucralose). sugar_g is confirmed zero per product label. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing ingredients. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero by product formulation. Glycemic index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. Sentinel values (-1): calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported on the product label or in the cross-referenced USDA analogous entry. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. Potassium_mg (~31 mg per 100 ml) and sodium_mg (~46 mg per 100 ml) are estimated from the declared electrolyte content on the product label and USDA analogous entries. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Beverages' as this is a liquid beverage product. Cuisine is classified as Singaporean as 100 Plus is an iconic Singapore-origin brand. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English (food_name), Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
